Name: curvature_aware_mixed_precision
Title: Curvature-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization via Spectral Decomposition and Stability Radii
Short Hypothesis: Optimal bit-width allocation in mixed-precision quantization can be determined by explicitly quantifying the relationship between Hessian conditioning, gradient covariance decay, and formalized stability radii, replacing heuristic proxies with deterministic spectral diagnostics.
Related Work: Existing MPQ methods (e.g., EMQ, InfoQ) rely on heuristic proxies or training-based search, which lack mathematical transparency and scalability. Recent work like AMP-ViT and SNIP introduces adaptive frameworks but does not formalize the connection between curvature metrics and precision routing. Our proposal bridges this gap by leveraging spectral decomposition and PAC-Bayes bounds to derive deterministic bit-width allocations, ensuring rigorous theoretical grounding and feasibility.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization (MPQ) offers a promising trade-off between model efficiency and accuracy, but existing methods rely on heuristic proxies or costly training-based search. We propose a curvature-aware precision routing framework that leverages spectral decomposition and formalized stability radii to optimize bit-width allocation. By quantifying Hessian conditioning and gradient covariance decay, our method replaces heuristic proxies with deterministic spectral diagnostics. We introduce a training-free protocol that computes PAC-Bayes generalization bounds and condition number constraints to derive optimal precision tiers. Experiments on vision transformers and LLMs demonstrate that our framework achieves superior accuracy-complexity trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art methods, with minimal computational overhead. Our approach ensures mathematical transparency, scalability, and reproducibility, making it ideal for resource-constrained deployments.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Validate the correlation between Hessian conditioning and optimal bit-width allocation on ImageNet using ResNet and MobileNet families. Metrics: Accuracy, BitOps, condition number.
Experiment 2: Compare our curvature-aware framework against EMQ and InfoQ on vision transformers (ViT, DeiT, Swin). Metrics: Accuracy, FLOPs, quantization error.
Experiment 3: Evaluate generalization bounds and stability radii on LLMs (Llama, GPT-2). Metrics: Perplexity, PAC-Bayes bounds, inference latency.
Experiment 4: Ablation study to isolate the impact of gradient covariance decay on bit-width allocation. Metrics: Sensitivity analysis, variance quantification.
Experiment 5: Benchmark computational overhead of our training-free protocol against existing MPQ methods. Metrics: Search time, memory usage, GPU utilization.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Spectral decomposition may introduce numerical instability for very large models. Mitigation: Use block-level decomposition and enforce condition number constraints.
Risk 2: Training-free proxies may not generalize to all architectures. Mitigation: Extend evaluations to diverse model families and datasets.
Limitation 1: Our framework assumes access to pre-trained models, limiting applicability to scenarios where training is unavoidable.

Limitation 2: The computational overhead of spectral diagnostics may increase for models with extreme depth or width. Mitigation: Optimize implementation using low-rank approximations and parallelized execution.
